description: Krita is amazing for digital painting and often outperforms Photoshop in that area. The switch is especially worth it for artists. Users who need advanced photo retouching or AI tools will miss Photoshop.
Kdenlive covers most of all editing needs, is resource-efficient, and stable. But it lacks the cloud and AI-powered tools that make Adobe convenient.
Great for general use. The switch is easy, provided you don't rely on complex macros or need full MS Office compatibility (formatting issues will happen).
Inkscape uses SVG as its native format and is extremely powerful for vector editing. However, the workflow is different from Adobe, and setting up for CMYK is more cumbersome.

Micro is a terminal editor that supports mouse input, making it work like a normal app. It is far more powerful than Notepad (syntax highlighting) but can be too much for non-programmers at first glance.
Blender is a powerhouse that does almost everything (3D, sculpting, video). The community is massive. Cinema4D is still more intuitive for new users, but Blender is catching up fast.
Open-source marketplaces respect your privacy, do not track you, and offer ad-free software. The downside: Popular commercial apps (Netflix, Spotify, etc.) are often not available.
Zen brings the modern UX design of Arc backed by the Firefox engine. The huge advantage: No Chromium and better privacy management. Since it's new, some minor features might still be missing.
